---
title: "Wine Chemistry and Correlates of Quality"
subtitle: "Stat 184 Final Project"
author: "Jason Kaufmann, Akshith Kachakayala, Joshua Tanyi"
date: "May 5, 2026"
date-modified: now
format:
  pdf:
    embed-resources: true
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: right
    fig-align: center
    cap-location: top
    link-external-newwindow: true
    code-overflow: wrap
execute: 
  warning: false
---

## Introduction

### Background

Whether it be to celebrate a special occasion or relax after a long day, wine is one of the most popular and versatile beverages in the world. According to a 2019 survey of US adults, about three-quarters of Americans have a glass of wine each week, with about 44% of the US population having multiple glasses each week. Among those who reported drinking wine regularly, the most common reasons included liking the taste, viewing it as a stress reliever, enjoying it with their food, and believing that it makes them happier (Gitlin, 2019). 

One common source of wine production is Vinho Verde in northwest Portugal. The region contains about 17,269 hectares of vineyards and produces about 100 million liters of wine each year (“Vinho Verde: Wines Full of Atlantic Freshness”, 2026). The popularity of wine around the world as well as the major role of Vinho Verde in the global wine market necessitate a deeper understanding of wines produced from this region.

### Objective

Although most people have an allegiance to either red or white wine, few are familiar with the chemical composition of either type. Our goal is to analyze how several variables relating to wine chemistry differ between red and white wine. Furthermore, we aim to find how characteristics of wine samples from the Vinho Verde region correlate to their quality, and we will identify whether this relationship changes based on the wine’s color.

### The Dataset

All data in this report comes from a study presented by Cortez et al. in 2009. The period of data collection was from May of 2004 to February of 2007. 1,599 samples of red wine and 4,898 samples of white wine were analyzed, with the chemical characteristics of each being determined by the iLab software. Each wine sample was tasted and evaluated by three qualified judges who rated it on a scale of one to ten (with ten being the highest quality and one being the lowest quality). These findings were summarized into two csv files: one for red wine and one for white wine (Cortez et al., 2009).

## Wine Chemistry by Color

### Similar Characteristics
#### Introduction
Some of the measured properties from the dataset are closely similar between both red and white wines. Without close investigation and analysis, we can easily make mistakes in which type of wine we are analysing which is why we will go in detail and closely analyze similar characteristics between both wines using analytical methods.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-similar-wine
#| tbl-cap: "Descriptive Statistics for pH and Alcohol showing similarities in Red and White Wine"
#| echo: false

#AUTHORS: JASON K. and JOSHUA T.
### Creating a Table of Descriptive Statistics that Contrasts Red and White Wine
## Step 1: Load necessary packages----
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(janitor)
## Step 2: Load the data----
redWineData <- 
  read.csv(file = "~/Desktop/wine+quality/winequality-red.csv",
           header = FALSE,
           sep = ";",
           skip = 1) # Omit the first row of column titles when importing data
whiteWineData <- 
  read.csv(file = "~/Desktop/wine+quality/winequality-white.csv",
           header = FALSE,
           sep = ";",
           skip = 1) # Omit the first row of column titles when importing data
## Step 3: Tidy the data for each type of wine----
redWineProgress <- redWineData |>
  rename( # Add descriptive column names
    "Fixed.Acidity" = V1,
    "Volatile.Acidity" = V2,
    "Citric.Acid" = V3,
    "Residual.Sugar" = V4,
    "Chlorides" = V5,
    "Free.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V6,
    "Total.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V7,
    "Density" = V8,
    "pH" = V9,
    "Sulphates" = V10,
    "Alcohol" = V11,
    "Quality" = V12
  ) |>
  mutate( # Create a column to label each sample as red wine
    color = "red" 
  )

whiteWineProgress <- whiteWineData |>
  rename(
    "Fixed.Acidity" = V1,
    "Volatile.Acidity" = V2,
    "Citric.Acid" = V3,
    "Residual.Sugar" = V4,
    "Chlorides" = V5,
    "Free.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V6,
    "Total.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V7,
    "Density" = V8,
    "pH" = V9,
    "Sulphates" = V10,
    "Alcohol" = V11,
    "Quality" = V12
  ) |>
  mutate( # Create a column to label each sample as white wine
    color = "white"
  )

## Step 4: Bind the dataframes for red and white wine----
wineData <- bind_rows(redWineProgress, whiteWineProgress)


wineSummary <- wineData |>
  group_by(color) |> # all subsequent operations will be performed for each color
  group_by(color) |> 
  summarise(
    minPH = min(pH, na.rm = TRUE),
    maxPH = max(pH, na.rm = TRUE),
    avgPH = mean(pH, na.rm = TRUE),
    medianPH = median(pH, na.rm = TRUE),
    stdevPH = sd(pH, na.rm = TRUE),
    
    minAlcohol = min(Alcohol, na.rm = TRUE),
    maxAlcohol = max(Alcohol, na.rm = TRUE),
    avgAlcohol = mean(Alcohol, na.rm = TRUE),
    medianAlcohol = median(Alcohol, na.rm = TRUE),
    stdevAlcohol = sd(Alcohol, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
longWineStats <- wineSummary |>
  rename(
    'Color' = color,
    'Min pH' = minPH,
    'Max pH' = maxPH,
    'Average pH' = avgPH,
    'Median pH' = medianPH,
    'St Dev pH' = stdevPH,
    'Min Alcohol (%)' = minAlcohol,
    'Max Alcohol (%)' = maxAlcohol,
    'Average Alcohol (%)' = avgAlcohol,
    'Median Alcohol (%)' = medianAlcohol,
    'St Dev Alcohol (%)' = stdevAlcohol
  ) |>
  select(-Color) |> # Remove the column containing the wine's color
  round(3) |> # Only show three digits for each value
  t() |> # Switch the rows and columns of the dataframe 
  as.data.frame() |>
  knitr::kable(
    caption = 'Descriptive Statistics for pH and Alcohol Concentrations for Red and White Wine',
    align = 'lcc', # Align the text
    col.names = c('', 'Red Wine', 'White Wine')
  ) |>
  kable_classic(
    lightable_options = 'striped' # Add light stripes to make the table more visually appealling
  ) |>
  footnote( # Add a footnote describing the data source
    general = 'Data sourced from Cortez et al., 2009',
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE # Fit the format on one line
  )
longWineStats
```
 @tbl-similar-wine summarizes the minimum, maximum, mean, median, and standard deviation for pH and alcohol content in red and white wines. The results show that both variables have very similar mean values, with only small differences between the two wine types. The similarity in median and variability further supports that pH and alcohol are consistent characteristics across red and white wines, especially when compared to other variables that show greater variation.
 
```{r}
#| label: fig-similar-wine
#| fig-cap: "Comparison of Mean pH and Alcohol Levels for Red and White Wine"
#| fig-alt: "Faceted bar chart comparing mean pH and mean alcohol levels for red and white wines. The bars are similar in height, indicating similar averages."
#| echo: false

#AUTHOR: JOSHUA TANYI
#Preparing the data for plotting
similarVisualData <- wineData |>
  select(color, pH, Alcohol) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(pH, Alcohol),
    names_to = "Characteristic",
    values_to = "Value"
  ) |>
  group_by(color, Characteristic) |>
  summarise(
    Average = mean(Value, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Plot showing similar values
ggplot(similarVisualData, aes(x = color, y = Average, fill = color)) +
  geom_col() +
  facet_wrap(~ Characteristic, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    x = "Wine Type",
    y = "Average Value",
    fill = "Wine Type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
@fig-similar-wine visually reinforces this pattern by showing the mean values of pH and alcohol for each wine type. The bars for red and white wines are nearly equal in height in both panels, highlighting the similarity in average values. Overall, these results suggest that pH and alcohol are consistent characteristics across wine types and are less useful for distinguishing between red and white wines compared to other variables.


### Stark Contrasts

```{r}
#| label: fig-sulfurDioxideBoxplot
#| fig-cap: "Boxplots showing total sulfur dioxide concentration for red and white wine samples"
#| fig-alt: "Boxplots for total sulfur dioxide concentration show a larger interquartile range, median, and range in white wine than in red wine"
#| fig-pos: "H"
#| fig-width: 8
#| echo: false

### Creating a Boxplot of Total Sulfur Dioxide Concentration by Wine Color
## Step 1: Load necessary packages----
library(tidyverse)
## Step 2: Load the data----
redWineData <- 
  read.csv(file = "~/Desktop/PSU/Sophomore Year Spring Semester/Stat 184/wine+quality (1)/winequality-red.csv",
           header = FALSE,
           sep = ";",
           skip = 1) # Omit the first row of column titles when importing data
whiteWineData <- 
  read.csv(file = "~/Desktop/PSU/Sophomore Year Spring Semester/Stat 184/wine+quality (1)/winequality-white.csv",
           header = FALSE,
           sep = ";",
           skip = 1) # Omit the first row of column titles when importing data
## Step 3: Tidy the data for each type of wine----
redWineProgress <- redWineData |>
  rename( # Add descriptive column names
    "Fixed.Acidity" = V1,
    "Volatile.Acidity" = V2,
    "Citric.Acid" = V3,
    "Residual.Sugar" = V4,
    "Chlorides" = V5,
    "Free.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V6,
    "Total.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V7,
    "Density" = V8,
    "pH" = V9,
    "Sulphates" = V10,
    "Alcohol" = V11,
    "Quality" = V12
  ) |>
  mutate( # Create a column to label each sample as red wine
    color = "red" 
  )

whiteWineProgress <- whiteWineData |>
  rename(
    "Fixed.Acidity" = V1,
    "Volatile.Acidity" = V2,
    "Citric Acid" = V3,
    "Residual.Sugar" = V4,
    "Chlorides" = V5,
    "Free.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V6,
    "Total.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V7,
    "Density" = V8,
    "pH" = V9,
    "Sulphates" = V10,
    "Alcohol" = V11,
    "Quality" = V12
  ) |>
  mutate( # Create a column to label each sample as white wine
    color = "white"
  )

## Step 4: Bind the dataframes for red and white wine----
wineData <- bind_rows(redWineProgress, whiteWineProgress)

## Step 5: Create the boxplot
sulfurDioxidePlot <- ggplot(
  data = wineData,
  mapping = aes(
    x = color, # Assign color to the x-axis
    y = Total.Sulfur.Dioxide, # Assign total sulfur dioxide to the y-axis
    color = color)
) +
  geom_boxplot(aes(fill = color), notch = TRUE, outlier.alpha = 0.5, color = "#000000") +
  # Fill each box based on the wine's color
  # Add in notches to easily compare the medians of each box
  # Make the outliers more transparent so that none of them are masked
  labs(
    title = "Sulfur Dioxide Concentration Based on Wine Color", # Add descriptive plot and axis titles
    x = "Wine Color",
    y = "Total Sulfur Dioxide (mg/dm^3)",
    subtitle = "Concentrations Measured by the iLab Software", # Provide context in the subtitle
    caption = "Source: Cortez et al., 2009" # Cite the data source in the caption
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") # Place the legend below the figure
sulfurDioxidePlot
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-vAcidityrSugar
#| tbl-cap: "Table of descriptive statistics for volatile acidity and residual sugar concentration in red and white wine"
#| tbl-pos: "H"
#| html-table-processing: none
#| echo: false

### Creating a Table of Descriptive Statistics that Contrasts Red and White Wine
## Step 1: Load necessary packages----
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(janitor)
## Step 2: Load the data----
redWineData <- 
  read.csv(file = "~/Desktop/PSU/Sophomore Year Spring Semester/Stat 184/wine+quality (1)/winequality-red.csv",
           header = FALSE,
           sep = ";",
           skip = 1) # Omit the first row of column titles when importing data
whiteWineData <- 
  read.csv(file = "~/Desktop/PSU/Sophomore Year Spring Semester/Stat 184/wine+quality (1)/winequality-white.csv",
           header = FALSE,
           sep = ";",
           skip = 1) # Omit the first row of column titles when importing data
## Step 3: Tidy the data for each type of wine----
redWineProgress <- redWineData |>
  rename( # Add descriptive column names
    "Fixed.Acidity" = V1,
    "Volatile.Acidity" = V2,
    "Citric.Acid" = V3,
    "Residual.Sugar" = V4,
    "Chlorides" = V5,
    "Free.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V6,
    "Total.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V7,
    "Density" = V8,
    "pH" = V9,
    "Sulphates" = V10,
    "Alcohol" = V11,
    "Quality" = V12
  ) |>
  mutate( # Create a column to label each sample as red wine
    color = "red" 
  )

whiteWineProgress <- whiteWineData |>
  rename(
    "Fixed.Acidity" = V1,
    "Volatile.Acidity" = V2,
    "Citric.Acid" = V3,
    "Residual.Sugar" = V4,
    "Chlorides" = V5,
    "Free.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V6,
    "Total.Sulfur.Dioxide" = V7,
    "Density" = V8,
    "pH" = V9,
    "Sulphates" = V10,
    "Alcohol" = V11,
    "Quality" = V12
  ) |>
  mutate( # Create a column to label each sample as white wine
    color = "white"
  )

## Step 4: Bind the dataframes for red and white wine----
wineData <- bind_rows(redWineProgress, whiteWineProgress)


wineSummary <- wineData |>
  group_by(color) |> # all subsequent operations will be performed for each color
  summarise(
    minVolatileAcidity = min(Volatile.Acidity, na.rm = TRUE), # Compute minimum volatile acidity concentration
    maxVolatileAcidity = max(Volatile.Acidity, na.rm = TRUE), # Compute maximum volatile acidity concentration
    avgVolatileAcidity = mean(Volatile.Acidity, na.rm = TRUE), # Compute average volatile acidity concentration
    medianVolatileAcidity = median(Volatile.Acidity, na.rm = TRUE), # Compute median volatile acidity concentration
    stdevVolatileAcidity = sd(Volatile.Acidity, na.rm = TRUE), # Compute st dev for volatile acidity concentration
    minResidualSugar = min(Residual.Sugar, na.rm = TRUE), # Compute minimum residual sugar concentration
    maxResidualSugar = max(Residual.Sugar, na.rm = TRUE), # Compute maximum residual sugar concentration
    avgResidualSugar = mean(Residual.Sugar, na.rm = TRUE), # Compute average residual sugar concentration
    medianResidualSugar = median(Residual.Sugar, na.rm = TRUE), # Compute median residual sugar concentration
    stdevResidualSugar = sd(Residual.Sugar, na.rm = TRUE), # Compute st dev for residual sugar concentration
  )
longWineStats <- wineSummary |>
  rename(
    'Color' = color, # Add descriptive column names
    'Min Volatile Acidity (g(acetic acid/L)' = minVolatileAcidity,
    'Max Volatile Acidity (g(acetic acid/L)' = maxVolatileAcidity,
    'Average Volatile Acidity (g(acetic acid/L)' = avgVolatileAcidity,
    'Median Volatile Acidity (g(acetic acid/L)' = medianVolatileAcidity,
    'St Dev Volatile Acidity (g(acetic acid/L)'= stdevVolatileAcidity,
    'Min Residual Sugar (g/L)' = minResidualSugar,
    'Max Residual Sugar (g/L)'= maxResidualSugar,
    'Average Residual Sugar (g/L)' = avgResidualSugar,
    'Median Residual Sugar (g/L)' = medianResidualSugar,
    'St Dev Residual Sugar (g/L)' = stdevResidualSugar
  ) |>
  select(-Color) |> # Remove the column containing the wine's color
  round(3) |> # Only show three digits for each value
  t() |> # Switch the rows and columns of the dataframe 
  as.data.frame() |>
  knitr::kable(
    caption = 'Descriptive Statistics for Volatile Acidity and Residual Sugar Concentrations for Red and White Wine',
    align = 'lcc', # Align the text
    col.names = c('', 'Red Wine', 'White Wine')
  ) |>
  kable_classic(
    lightable_options = 'striped' # Add light stripes to make the table more visually appealling
  ) |>
  footnote( # Add a footnote describing the data source
    general = 'Data sourced from Cortez et al., 2009',
    footnote_as_chunk = TRUE # Fit the format on one line
  )
longWineStats
```

##  Correlates of Quality

### Strongest Predictors of Quality

Text

### Weakest Predictors of Quality

Text

### Varying Predictors of Quality by Color

Text

## Conclusion

### Takeaways

Text

### Next Steps

Text

## Conclusion

{{< pagebreak >}}  

## Code Appendix (IMPORTANT: ECHO NEEDS TO BE SET TO TRUE BEFORE SUBMITTING. I set it to false since we're still editing the file and it will be a hassle to see such a large chunk of code in the document while editing. Edit can only be made in qmd file.)

```{r}
#| label: codeAppend
#| ref-label: !expr knitr::all_labels()
#| echo: false
#| eval: false

```
